coredata.dc:title: Swelling, pressure evolution and aging in high-silicon/ graphite composite lithium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description:
The expansion of particles continues to present a challenge to the mechanical integrity of silicon-containing anodes, especially as their elemental silicon content has continued to increase. By applying external mechanical pressure, the anode and cell stack can be stabilized. Therefore, in this study, the silicon materials SiO
x
and Si-C were investigated in a composite anode with graphite under varying external mechanical resistance, which influences pressure development. By performing a long-term aging study at the pouch cell level with an NMC811 cathode, microstructure degradation, rather than the material used (Si-C or SiO
x
) or its content, was identified as the main weak point. The two components of the composite anode, namely graphite and silicon, contributed to swelling and degradation behavior in different ways and depending on the bracing condition. This observation was made using a variety of experimental methods, including the use of a powerful, self-developed cell press to study electromechanical effects such as swelling, post-mortem analysis, and differential voltage analysis. In-depth analysis of the correlations identified pressure as the controlling physical quality. These results demonstrate that swelling, pressure, and aging must be considered together in silicon-containing cells, as their correlations impact energy density and lifetime performance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrode and pouch cell thicknesses (uncycled and at EoT) for the investigated silicon-containing pouch cells. It should be noted that measurement by micrometer is somewhat inaccurate because the electrode is relaxed after cell opening. The results from this table should therefore be viewed in qualitative terms and are intended to provide an indication of anode and cathode contributions to the measured growth at the full cell level.

head.ce:title: Swelling, pressure evolution and aging in high-silicon/ graphite composite lithium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The expansion of particles continues to present a challenge to the mechanical integrity of silicon-containing anodes, especially as their elemental silicon content has continued to increase. By applying external mechanical pressure, the anode and cell stack can be stabilized. Therefore, in this study, the silicon materials SiO
x
and Si-C were investigated in a composite anode with graphite under varying external mechanical resistance, which influences pressure development. By performing a long-term aging study at the pouch cell level with an NMC811 cathode, microstructure degradation, rather than the material used (Si-C or SiO
x
) or its content, was identified as the main weak point. The two components of the composite anode, namely graphite and silicon, contributed to swelling and degradation behavior in different ways and depending on the bracing condition. This observation was made using a variety of experimental methods, including the use of a powerful, self-developed cell press to study electromechanical effects such as swelling, post-mortem analysis, and differential voltage analysis. In-depth analysis of the correlations identified pressure as the controlling physical quality. These results demonstrate that swelling, pressure, and aging must be considered together in silicon-containing cells, as their correlations impact energy density and lifetime performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the race for maximum energy density, silicon–graphite composite anodes represent a promising transition technology towards pure silicon anodes, Li metal, or anode-free cell designs [1–6]. Particularly in the automotive industry, high energy densities are needed in order to improve the drivable range, and the silicon content has thus increased [7]. Increasing the silicon content in composite anodes with graphite, however, leads to practical issues such as high-volume expansion at the particle level, which not only deteriorates cyclic aging performance of the anode [8–10] but also propagates to the level of the full cell [11,12]. Thus, previous research has been conducted to stabilize the expanding silicon at the particle level. Various technologies have been proposed in the literature, including (modified) silicon oxide [13,14] or carbon-stabilized silicon (Si-C), either designed as a so-called core–shell structure or yolk-shell structure [15]. In core–shell structures, the carbon is also stretched upon silicon expansion, whereas in the yolk-shell structure, void spaces are provided at the particle level and the volume change is buffered [15].

Another way of stabilizing the anode is by applying external mechanical pressure. Several studies have investigated the effects of external mechanical pressure and found that pressure has a large impact on not only electrochemical [16–20] but also lifetime performance [21–26]. The effects of pressure are expected to be even higher for silicon-containing cells, as the pressure increase is higher due to the greater expansion of silicon [11,27]. If certain limits are not exceeded, the application of external mechanical pressure has been reported to have a positive effect, as layer delamination due to gassing and expansion, electrical contact loss, and coating detachment are improved [10,18,28,29]. Compared to graphite-based Li-ion cells, silicon-containing cells exhibit more complex expansion and pressure evolution, which poses further challenges regarding their integration into batteries [11,27,30]. Furthermore, recent studies have observed interactions between applied external mechanical pressure and electrode morphology, affecting electrochemical performance via porosity change [12,31,32]. Investigations with the discrete element method (DEM) also indicate that the reversible and irreversible expansion behavior is mainly related to the morphology of the electrode [33]. Especially during the first cycles, the particle rearrangement seems to influence the expansion behavior [33,34].

While the studies mentioned above have mostly focused on a single aspect, for example graphite-based Li-ion cells, the interrelationship of swelling, pressure increase, and aging in silicon-containing cells is still not fully understood. In these studies, different silicon-containing active materials with varying contents and compression conditions were employed, making comparisons difficult and implying a need for further experimental effort. Furthermore, testing at the level of the full cell has been noted by researchers as particularly important regarding silicon-containing anodes [35–38]. As the Li and electrolyte reservoirs in coin cells are not limiting, testing may lead to better performance in coin cells as compared to full cells [35–38]. In addition, the stiff housing of coin cells constrains the anode mechanically and thus influences expansion [38] in an undefined way. It is thus desirable to investigate state-of-the-art silicon-containing active materials at the pouch cell level with more than one layer, using a precise experimental configuration and a systematic experimental design.

Thus, in this study, three different silicon anode technologies were investigated in a pouch cell with a high-nickel cathode (NMC811) and compared to a pouch cell with a graphite anode only. The consideration of a pouch cell with several layers and realistic pressure levels provided a holistic image of the practical performance of the applied materials. In addition to the baseline graphite pouch cell, two anodes with SiO
x
material but different contents (22 and 34 wt%) and one anode with silicon–carbon material (Si-C, 34 wt%) were investigated. For the application of external pressure as well as mechanical resistance, an in-house-developed, electronically controlled, high-precision cell press was employed. With this cell press, accurately defined mechanical resistance could be applied and varied, allowing not only the analysis of the mechanical comparability of different cell chemistries but also an assessment and comparison based on the literature.

In contrast to previous studies [10,38,39], a central goal of this study was to understand the effects of pressure when comparing different (silicon-containing) active material technologies. In addition, the link between swelling and the observed aging mechanism was analyzed and interpreted in more depth. Analyzing the relevant literature [11,21–23,40–44], the total cell swelling can be divided into reversible and irreversible thickness changes of the cell components. The irreversible cell growth occurs during formation and increases further during subsequent cycling as a result of cell degradation mechanisms [10,22,24,41,42,45]. One of the main reasons for the increase appears to be the growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) [22,41]. Similar to the miscellaneous aging mechanism [46,47], the exact irreversible growth mechanisms and their interplay with degradation are still not fully understood and are thus subject to further research. However, the broad consensus of these studies is that irreversible swelling is closely linked to the SoH (state of health) evolution. For this reason, the extent of the different contributions to irreversible (as well as reversible) swelling is also dependent on the temperature [23,42,45], current rate [23,48], SoC (state of charge), DoD (depth of discharge) [23,49], as well as the selection of the active material and thus particle and electrode composition [41,49,50]. The irreversible swelling behavior is therefore often highly non-linear and is related to the aging mechanism that occurs in each case [23,24,34]. Pressure evolution as a result of irreversible swelling affects the integration of a battery system and thus leads to practical issues in battery development.

Regarding the experimental parameters, each cell chemistry was investigated under different pressure conditions during cycling. During subsequent cycling, each cell was braced by a constant mechanical resistance, as cells are typically braced and not operated under perfectly constant pressure conditions (e.g. stiff battery housing). It was found that consideration of swelling, pressure, and aging is necessary for the design of a high-energy battery with silicon anodes. By applying the knowledge thus gained, the mechanical boundary conditions of a cell can be designed so that the cell operates at optimal pressure in terms of performance and aging behavior while maintaining the mechanical integrity of the battery housing.

In total, four types of anode electrodes and one cathode were developed and built. For the anode, a pure graphite anode, a SiO
x
–graphite composite anode with 22 wt% and 34 wt% SiO
x
contents, and another composite anode with a silicon–carbon composite material (Si-C, 34 wt%) were developed. Both silicon-containing particles (SiO
x
vs. Si-C) have a comparable gravimetric capacity. The electrode compositions are shown in Table 1.

Regarding the particle size and shape, the graphite particles typically had a round to flake-form particle shape. Instead, the SiO
x
and Si-C particles exhibited a diamond-shaped form and were much smaller in size. The cross section shown in Fig. 4 (pristine electrode) illustrates the electrode morphology. A carbon structure is visible on the Si-C particle, which mechanically stabilizes the particle in the event of a volume change and is thus also intended to improve the electrical conductivity of the pure silicon. However, the SiO
x
particles were also modified by the manufacturer by applying a carbon-based coating to mechanically stabilize the particles and improve their cycling stability. In the cathode, a high-nickel NMC811 was applied.

From the electrodes, four types of pouch cells were built. The electrodes and pouch cells were developed and manufactured with Cidetec (Spain). In each pouch cell, 10 layers of a double-sided coated cathode and 11 layers of a double-sided coated anode were used. The same cathode was used in all cells. The electrodes were punched and stacked, with one separator sheet in between. The spatial dimensions of the cathode were 98 mm by 59 mm. The anode dimensions were 100 mm by 61 mm. The stacked pouch cells were heated overnight in a dedicated oven (Binder, Germany) at 80 °C in vacuum. Subsequently, the cells were filled with 13 ml of electrolyte (Solvionic, France) with the formulation EC:DMC (1:1, vol.), 1M LiP
F
6
, 1 wt% VC, 1 wt% TFSI, and 10 wt% FEC. After filling in an argon-filled glovebox (MBraun, Germany), a vacuum of 50 mbar was created for soaking. All relevant specifications of the pouch cells employed are summarized in Table 2. In the following descriptions, the type of pouch cell has been named according to the designations in Tables 1 and 2.

The high-precision cell press is shown in Fig. 1 and has been presented in detail in a previous report [31]. The most important technical specifications are presented in Table 3. The press is driven by air pressure and electronically controlled by software for high-accuracy force regulation and measurement. The cell is placed between two moving compression plates, and the pressure is applied by the press while all thickness changes are measured by the three sensors mounted on the press. The accuracy of the force and the thickness sensor is below 1% and thus highly accurate [51,52]. A constant pressure or a mechanical resistance against the cell with varying pressure can be applied. Cycling under applied mechanical resistance rather than constant pressure is considered to be closer to reality, as cells in application rarely experience constant pressure conditions but rather a pressure increase dependent on the mechanical boundary conditions around the cell. In particular, the accurate setting and variation of mechanical resistance are considered improvements compared to previous studies, as the exact mechanical resistance in SI units was clearly defined in the preset configuration, which increases the comparability of the results, such as the pressure increase during expansion. Based on the infinitely resolvable, precise adjustment of the mechanical resistance, optimization problems with a variation of mechanical boundary conditions can also be solved in principle [22]. Due to the very stable structure, high pressures of up to 3 MPa (our cell format) can also be investigated.

All electrical and mechanical tests were conducted in a controlled-temperature chamber (Memmert GmbH, Germany) at a constant temperature of 25 °C. The entire cell press was placed inside the chamber. Cycling was conducted using a BaSyTec CTS electrical cell test system (Basytec GmbH, Germany).

The cycle profiles used in this study are summarized in Table 4. Before beginning formation, 12 h were spent soaking. Every cell test began with an initial check-up cycle at the beginning of life (BoL). Before each current pulse for the determination of the internal resistance (IR), the capacity was measured such that the SoC remained at 50% during the progressing aging. The alternating procedure of check-up cycles and cycle phases was repeated until the end of test (EoT) which was defined as SoH = 70% in this study.

The external mechanical resistance was applied on the cell before the test, resulting in pressure relaxation until a force equilibrium had been reached between the cell and the applied mechanical resistance. Three different mechanical resistances, namely soft, medium, and high stiffness, were applied to each investigated cell chemistry. The values are summarized in Table 5.
A
cell
represents the compressed area of the cell and was calculated from the length and width of the cathode, as the cathode had the smallest area of all cell components and thus determined the maximum pressure along the cell thickness. The value of
k
m e c h
was normalized by the initial cell thickness
d
0
(reference) for each cell type according to the equation in the last column in Table 5.

For post-mortem analysis, the cells were first deep discharged to 2 V. The cells were disassembled in an argon-filled glovebox (MBraun, Germany) after cycling. The samples for the SEM were washed in DMC to remove residual conducting salts and other residues. Device Mira3 from company TESCAN (Czech Republic) was used for the SEM recordings. The electrode cross sections were recorded with a view field of 250 or 300 μ m at 15 kV. The detail views were recorded with a view field of 25 μ m at 15 kV.

The measurement results were analyzed using the equations shown in Table 6. The overall thickness change of the cell
Δ
d
c e l l
was measured by the cell press and the initial cell thickness
d
0 , c e l l
at the mechanically retarded state immediately before the first check-up began at 0% SoC. For all calculations, it was assumed that length and width remained the same and the cell only expanded or was compressed in the direction of the cell’s thickness. The pressure was evaluated in the cell’s thickness direction. The calculated energy throughput includes both the cycle phases and the check-up cycles.

For each chemistry, three bracing conditions were tested. As shown in Fig. 2a–d, for all chemistries, the higher mechanical resistance (and thus higher pressure development) was disadvantageous, as the achievable energy throughput was decreased. Furthermore, the graphite cell was much more robust compared to the silicon-containing cells during cycling, as more energy was put through at each bracing condition. The graphite cells were stopped after > 800 cycles and had not reached the prospective SoH of 70% at that point. While the graphite SoH development plateaued after an initial drop of the SoH, the silicon-containing cell’s SoH continued to fade progressively. The cells cycled at the stiff bracing condition even showed sudden-death behavior as the SoH curve changed its slope for the worse beyond SoH = 80%. As already observed in the literature [10,22,24,41,42,45] and now also observed, the irreversible swelling correlates strongly with the SoH. Comparing the different types of pouch cells investigated in this study, the cycle number and thus energy throughput differed strongly depending on not only the cell chemistry but also the bracing condition; thus, the measures were analyzed via the SoH in the following sections. As expected, swelling clearly increased with increasing silicon content.

Of note, considering IR evolution, the evolution between graphite and silicon-containing cells was vice versa: For the silicon-containing cells, the increase in relative IR was higher at the softly braced cell with lower pressure development. In the soft bracing, the graphite cell even demonstrated an improvement in the IR during the first cycles and remained the smallest compared to SoH in other measurements. In general, the IR increase over the lifetime was much higher for silicon-containing cells. The observed differences between graphite and silicon-containing cells are thus discussed in the following.

As shown in the third row in Fig. 2, until an SoH of 70%, the SiO
x
22 cell swelled by approximately 13%, the SiO
x
34 cell by approximately 14%, and the Si-C 34 cell by approximately 16%, with an increasing slope beyond an SoH of 80%. As SiO
x
particles expanded reversibly by up to approximately 120% during each cycle [12,53], they may have lost electrical connection and increased the electrode thickness if the particles were fully charged [10,34]. As shown by Müller et al. [9], it is likely that the large free expansion in the soft configuration of the silicon left void spaces in the deformed electrode network that did not fully reform. This process would have loosened the microstructure and might also have increased the free surface for further SEI growth [34]. A created void space is thus an additional explanation for irreversible cell growth. The notion of a void space and thus its existence have been previously validated by modeling the reversible anode expansion at different pressures [12,31]. However, the void space may also explain the deteriorated IR during the soft bracing, as the deterioration of electrical conductivity was twofold: First, the particles were less connected to the electrode network when discharged, as the void space remained but the particles shrank. Second, the ion paths were increased and thus lengthened in the heavily grown electrode. The pressure reduced both the void space and the electrode thickness, thus improving the electrical connection with the electrode network, as clearly shown in Fig. 2e–h. An improved ohmic resistance in silicon-containing cells due to pressure has previously been reported in the literature as well [38]. Overall, the relative IR increase was much higher in the silicon-containing cells compared to the graphite cells.

At high pressure (30 and 60 MPa bracing), the swelling decreased strongly, which has been observed previously [10,54]. As explanation, the notion of softer silicon particles adapting to the electrode microstructure has already been used as an explanation for the observed pressure dependency [31]. The silicon-containing cells even shrank slightly after some cycles, whereas the graphite cells monotonically grew, indicating rearrangements or even breakage of the silicon particles into the pore and void spaces, thus decreasing the anode thickness. The phenomenon of shrinkage has previously been observed [27,32].

Of note, the pouch cells with the higher silicon-material contents of 34 wt% in the very stiff configuration (60 MPa) performed better compared to the less-stiff one (30 MPa) with lower pressure evolution. Although low pressure seems to have been the optimum for lifetime performance according to the measurements, the improved performance at very high pressure suggests that for higher silicon contents, the pressure had a stabilizing effect. For example, the pressure may have delayed the detachment of the coating which is shown in a later section or cracks that had appeared were healed again [29]. Different from the SiO
x
, the irreversible swelling of the cell with the Si-C particle increased its slope, potentially because no buffering Li
4
SiO4 matrix was available which is formed during the first lithiation of SiO
x
[53]. The degrading Si-C particle apparently revealed a pure silicon surface upon particle degradation, which led to additional volume expansion when the reaction with the Li inventory and electrolyte occurred and could lead to the development of a thickness runaway behavior after further cyclization. A thickness runaway behavior could be triggered either by an exhausted FEC content [41] or by a destroyed carbon structure or graphite coating [55]. The core–shell technology of the Si-C effectively decreased the volume expansion when compared to the SiO
x
particle at BoL. A decline in the mechanical integrity of the applied carbon could thus also explain the increased swelling after cycling compared to SiO
x
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pressure and cycle number
ce:section.ce:para: The number of cycles determines how often the active material particle expands and contracts and is thus an important measure of the mechanical load the particle experiences in long-term battery operation [22].

In the first row in Fig. 3, the SoH has been correlated with the maximum pressure reached during the cycling at the different bracing conditions, evaluated for certain cycle numbers. After 100 cycles, for all investigated cells, only a slight negative influence of the pressure is visible. A linear fit has been created between the data points to highlight the correlation. The correlation became much more negative after more cycles, revealing the connection between the SoH and repeated cycling under applied external pressure. The negative correlation was much more pronounced for the silicon-containing anodes compared to the graphite cell: not only was the slope steeper, but the cycles necessary for the same degradation were also much shorter. In combination with high cycle numbers, the pressure apparently promoted the deterioration of the mechanical stability of the silicon-containing electrode. Due to the relative cyclic stability of the graphite pouch cell and the fact that the same cathode was used in all investigated cells, the cathode was excluded as a factor limiting the SoH performance in this case. As discussed in the previous section, the silicon cells with 34% silicon material (Fig. 3c–d) showed a stabilizing effect at high pressure, thus the linear fit showed no good fit for maximum pressure reached and high cycle number. However, in total, the strong negative effect of the high pressure remains obvious.

At BoL, the silicon-containing cells exhibited a slightly lower IR compared to the graphite cell (Fig. 3e–h). Because all electrodes had the same loading and similar composition, this effect was likely due to the thinner electrode thickness of the silicon-containing cells. The pouch cell with the Si-C particle exhibited the lowest absolute IR, as it had the lowest anode thickness of all investigated cells. This initial advantage of the silicon-containing cells, however, was already put into perspective after 100 cycles, as the deterioration of the IR was much worse, showing a behavior approximately in correlation with the SoH deterioration. At BoL, the pressure seems to have had a slight positive influence on the IR of the graphite pouch cell, while the silicon-containing cells showed relative insensitivity to pressure. As the same cathode and separator types were used, these differences must have stemmed from the anode. Due to the high loading and thus the thick electrode, it is assumed that the coarse-grained graphite electrode was packed more densely and the electrical conductivity was improved at higher pressure.

All in all, the investigated cells were relatively insensitive to the applied external pressure at BoL during the discharge pulse, which was similar to that observed in the literature [20]. Different from the graphite cell, the influence of pressure on the silicon-containing cells changed from insensitive to positive with further cycling, indicating a reduction of the IR with increasing pressure. This observation has been analyzed in the previous section and was linked to the decreased swelling and void spaces upon increased pressure. The IR of the cells with higher SiO
x
contents showed a neutral correlation with the maximum pressure during further cycling; however, considering the strong capacity fade of the cells, the lack of deterioration of the IR was unexpected. This can be used to interpret that the cycle number was not sufficiently high to harm the mechanical integrity of the actual active material particle. The root cause for the observed strong SoH loss is thus assumed to have been different from particle degradation, for example the mechanical integrity of the electrode structure or SEI-growth [56].

The irreversible swelling shown in Fig. 2i–l has been separated from the reversible SoC stroke shown in Fig. 3m–p, but the sum of both is the total swelling according to Fig. 2. While the SoC stroke of graphite was relatively insensitive to the external pressure (shown in Fig. 3m), the reversible SoC stroke of the silicon anodes was heavily reduced at higher pressure (Fig. 3n–p). The pressure-dependent displacement of swelling within the electrode plane is expected to lead to an increase in interparticle stresses. For a fixed cycle number, the negative correlation remained after further cycling and did not change its slope considerably. However, for all cells (also graphite) the reversible SoC stroke was reduced after cycling. This result correlated with the SoH loss, was in accordance with the literature, and is due to loss of active material in the anode [23,45]. The graphite particles apparently played a dominant role in the irreversible growth, which was not obvious when only the total swelling was considered according to Fig. 2i–l. Notably, the graphite cell grew irreversibly, nearly similar to the silicon-containing cells. It can thus be concluded that the observed higher total swelling of the silicon-containing pouch cells in Fig. 2 was driven mainly by their higher SoC stroke, shown in Fig. 3m–p.

Another similarity between the irreversible swelling of the graphite and the silicon-containing pouch cells is strong pressure-dependent reduction of the swelling, similar to what was observed for the irreversible swelling in the literature as well [10,22]. However, the size of the irreversible swelling correlated with the SoH loss, as it strongly increased after cycling. This is thought to increase interparticle stresses in addition to the reversible expansion and could therefore be related to the SoH. Of note, some irreversible cell growth had already occurred during the first cycle after formation (irreversible swelling at BoL: Appendix A, S.9).

As the slopes of both reversible and irreversible growth were relatively invariant to the cycle number, the decrease is assumed to have been due to the electrochemical loss of entire particles (electrical or ionic disconnection) within the anode structure due to high interparticular stress. As observed, the disconnection was slightly improved by high external pressure, as shown by the SoH/RI performance increase at very high pressure. The effect is shown in another representation in Fig. 3q–t via the ETP: Because higher pressure solely led to increased degradation and a higher IR for the graphite cell, the realized ETP at a fixed cycle number decreased with increasing pressure. The silicon-containing cells, on the other hand, showed improved realized ETP with increasing pressure as the IR is improved as well, which led to higher energy throughput per cycle.
The in-depth analysis presented in Section 3.1 can be summarized by the following conclusions:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Due to their high volume share, graphite particles still seem to play a dominant role in the irreversible growth observed even in silicon-containing cells. Silicon-containing particles increase the reversible SoC stroke dependent on the silicon content, mostly increasing the overall swelling of silicon-containing cells by this contribution.

Reversible and irreversible swelling are strongly decreased by pressure at a fixed SoH/cycle number for silicon-containing cells. On pure graphite, the pressure only decreases their irreversible growth, while their reversible expansion is almost insensitive.

Due to the thinner electrode thickness, silicon-containing cells exhibit a lower IR at BoL but deteriorate at much worse levels compared to graphite due to the large mechanical expansion of the silicon-containing particles. Void spaces between the silicon particles in loosened or expanded silicon-containing anodes seem to be compressed at higher pressure, improving the electrical connection of the particles in the electrode network and thus reducing the IR. This effect can also improve the cyclic SoH evolution and eventually lead to a higher realized energy throughput at elevated pressure.

The strong capacity fade, likely from active material loss, that was observed in the silicon-containing cells compared to the pure graphite cell is assumed to have been due to the deterioration of the anode microstructure (e.g. loss of electrical/ionic connection) rather than particle deterioration, as correlations of swelling with pressure and cycle number showed no changing slope. The pressure-dependent compression/ displacement of the swelling into the pores and into the electrode plane obviously increases the interparticle stresses in the electrode plane and can thus explain the pressure-dependent aging. The number of cycles determines the frequency and thus explains its negative influence on the electrode structure. The effects are observed equally for the graphite and silicon containing cells, but are amplified by the large SoC stroke of the silicon containing cells and increasing elemental silicon content.

ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 shows the SiO
x
and Si-C electrodes, each with 34 wt%, that were harvested from the cycled cells during the cell teardown. In the pristine, uncycled electrode in Fig. 4a–b, both electrodes show sharply edged particles, and the porous network is also visible (black background). In the detailed view in Fig. 4g and h, the SiO
x
, Si-C, and graphite particles are labeled. Again, the particles exhibit sharp edges, and the pore space is clearly recognizable.

Fig. 4c shows the SiO
x
34 electrode cycled at soft bracing (2 MPa). The electrode thickness has been heavily increased. The electrode and pouch cell thicknesses are shown in Table 7. Comparing Fig. 4c with Fig. 4a, it is still possible to recognize the remaining pore volume (black); however, their shape and size have changed. Different from the pores visible in (a), the visible pores in (c) mostly have the shape of the particle they surround, and they appear rather like void spaces in a loosened electrode structure, as though each particle has had its own room to expand. In addition, the SiO
x
particles (white) appear rounder compared to the sharp, splintered, or diamond-shaped particles in the pristine electrode shown in (a). Even within spots of agglomerated SiO
x
particles in (a), pores and cavities remain visible. These tend to be filled in the cycled electrode shown in (c). Furthermore, greyish veils and hems are also visible within the SiO
x
particles and are clear signs of a beginning particle degradation. The particles themselves seem to be relatively undamaged. Arrow 2 in Fig. 4i indicates a visible hem at the SiO
x
particle edge. An EDX (energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) analysis comparing the particle center with the particle edge shows increased F and P contents in this area, which are possibly components of the SEI (see Appendix A, S.10). Grayish residues observed on the anode surface, which could indicate Li plating (see Appendix A, S.11 left column), could also have contributed to the strong increase in cell thickness.

The electrode structure of the cells cycled at the stiff bracing (60 MPa; Fig. 4e–f) looks completely different from those at the soft bracing: there is no visible pore volume at all. The growth of the silicon-containing particles has severely clogged the remaining pore volume as they could not grow in the direction of cell thickness. As there is no volume left for the electrolyte due to the pore blockage, the anode could dry out and thus explain the sudden cell death of the highly strained cell. Void spaces are also visible around the particles, but they are much less pronounced compared to the soft bracing. Severe cracks that pass through the electrode are visible, indicating high interparticular stress. However, the pressure seems to hold the particles and thus the electrode morphology together, as evidenced by the small residual electrolyte paths shown in Fig. 4e–f and the presumably still existing electrical contacting of the particles in the stiffly braced condition. The high capacity loss can therefore be explained by ionic disconnection due to electrode densification and plastic particle deformation. Both the ionic disconnection and the clogging are assumed have increased the IR, as measured by comparison to the graphite cell.

In addition, the detailed view of the aged electrode in Fig. 4i shows how the SiO
x
particles have been deformed. Due to the external pressure in combination with apparent particle growth, the particles have adapted to the electrode microstructure, forming a sharp edge, as shown by arrow 1 in Fig. 4i. No such changes are visible in the graphite particles. Regarding the Si-C electrode depicted on the right in Fig. 4, similar observations can be made in principle: The softly braced electrode (Fig. 4d) shows a loosened microstructure, while the stiffly braced electrode (Fig. 4f) seems heavily compressed, leaving no visible pore space but only the void space from the particle expansion. Arrow 1 in Fig. 4j shows how the Si-C particle has also adapted to the microstructure, and arrow 2 also shows a hem.

Different to other studies, in our study, however, in general, the particles seem to be in a state of mechanical integrity and are not pulverized [10]. The capacity loss is thus assumed to have stemmed mostly from the loss of active Li building SEI and the loss of active material due to the electrical and ionic disconnection of particles from the repeated mechanical expansion. The decreasing reversible expansion shown in Fig. 3m–p supports this hypothesis, since electrically or ionically decontacted particles no longer participate in the expansion. The presumed dominant aging mechanisms are summarized schematically in Fig. 5a–c. During the cell teardown, the coating of the cells cycled at high pressure (stiff bracing) detached completely from the copper, which was interpreted as a sign of high interparticular stress. Images are shown in Appendix A (S.11). An over-compressed separator can be ruled out as the cause of the sudden death, since higher pressures than 3 MPa seem to be required and the anode would have aged less in this case [10,57,58].

The observations of the microstructure using SEM and the visual inspection of the electrodes confirm the interpretations from the test data presented in Section 3.1 for both the SiO
x
and the Si-C electrodes: while the particles themselves seem to have retained their integrity so far, the anode microstructure has deteriorated.

The SoC stated in Fig. 6 was defined between the fixed voltage limits of 2.8 and 4.2 V for each cell shown and SoH. In the left column in Fig. 6, the DV (differential voltage) curves at BoL and after the first cycles (30 cycles) are shown for the soft (dashed line) and the stiff (solid line) bracings. On the right, all four investigated chemistries have been analyzed in 10% SoH steps until reaching EoT.

At BoL and after a capacity fade of 5%, the influence of the bracing and thus increased pressure seems to have been negligible for the graphite cell shown in Fig. 6a. The first peak around 18% SoC stemmed from the characteristic voltage stage of graphite before it was completely discharged and can be recognized in the DV curve, as graphite exhibits distinct voltage plateaus upon charge and discharge [59,60]. As the peak has shifted to the left at SoH = 95%, this stage was apparently reached at a higher SoC, supporting the hypothesis of active material loss at the anode. The active material loss constitutes approximately 2%–3% of the observed capacity loss during the first 30 cycles. The rest (ca. 2%–3%) is attributed to the loss of active Li, as the cathode valley shows no significant shift. The first valley from left is attributed to the cathode, as high nickel cathodes show a distinct dip in the cathode voltage in that SoC region [12]. The active Li was most likely consumed by SEI growth, as this effect can be observed by strong, measured irreversible swelling during the first 5% of capacity fade (Fig. 2i).

After further cycling of the graphite cell shown in Fig. 6b, the aging mode seems not to have significantly changed depending on the bracing condition, as the curves of the soft (dashed) and the stiff bracing (solid) remain mostly overlapped. However, the characteristic peaks are significantly more pronounced in the softly braced cell compared to the stiffly braced cell at SoH of 90 and 85%, respectively. This effect indicates that the active material was much more heterogeneously lithiated [61,62]. At 80% SoH and thus after further aging, the difference evened out somewhat. Such heterogeneity at increased pressure has been observed previously [63] and is likely due to inhomogeneous aging of a stiffly braced cell. Due to the very high pressure, the cell components, particularly the separator, are heavily compressed. Depending on the distribution of the current density, increased aging and swelling may occur locally. This leads to locally increased pressures, which in turn affect the current density locally, resulting in inhomogeneous aging [10]. During further cycling until SoH = 80%, the characteristic graphite peak on the right has shifted further to the left by approximately 6%, clearly attributing this capacity loss to anode active material. The remaining capacity loss (ca. 14%) is thus due to the loss of active Li and cathode degradation. Loss of active Li probably constitutes the majority of the remaining capacity loss, as the irreversible swelling showed steep progress until SoH of 85% (see Fig. 2i) for both stiff and soft configurations and the characteristic cathode valley (first valley from left) does not significantly change its peak position.

Analyzing the SiO
x
22 cell in Fig. 6c, much more change is visible after 30 cycles. Stark changes in the positions of the peaks can be observed at SoH = 95%. As the cell contained silicon, at first the graphite was delithiated, and only afterwards the silicon [64]. In Fig. 6c, this characteristic lithiation behavior of silicon-containing graphite-composite cells can also be observed: the characteristic sharp peaks created by the four stages of graphite [60] are visible in Fig. 6a and reappear on the left half (region of 100 to 50% SoC) in Fig. 6c. The delithiation range of graphite is indicated by I in Fig. 6, the range of silicon by II. The last graphite peak appears at approximately 50% SoC in the BoL DV curve, which indicates that both components contributed equally to the total capacity. After a loss of 5% of the capacity of the pouch cell, the three particularly sharp peaks that reflect the graphite have shifted to the left by approximately 5%. This effect is a clear indication that the observed loss in this case originated mostly from the graphite share (loss of active material) within the composite anode. The capacity share of graphite on the full cell thus changed from ca. 50% to only approximately 45% (SiO
x
: ca. 50% to 55%). This result supports the previous analyses from Sections Section 3.1.2 (swelling measurements) and 3.2.1 (cell teardown): The electrode structure was mechanically loosened upon cycling, leading to the electrical disconnection of particles. The DV analysis indicates that mostly graphite particles were apparently affected by the electrical disconnection as the particles were forced apart by the expansion of the SiO
x
. As can be seen in Fig. 3m–p, the reversible swelling fraction decreases as graphite ceases to participate in the expansion. The electrically decontacted particles could be observed in the SEM (Fig. 4c–d). The phenomenon was similar in the soft and stiff bracing; however, it was slightly more pronounced in the soft bracing, in which the anode was allowed to expand further. In the stiffly braced cell, in which the particles tend to be pressed against each other, ionic decontacting is more likely to occur due to the densification of the electrodes and the plastic deformation of the particles.

During further cycling until SoH = 70%, shown in Fig. 6d, the DV curves of the soft and stiff bracing have begun to grow apart. In the heavily breathing, softly braced cell, the graphite capacity losses continued to increase as the graphite peaks seemed to move further to the left. In the stiffly braced cell, however, the capacity ratio between graphite and silicon does not change, as the pronounced graphite peak at ca. 75% SoC shows. This could be due the observed stabilizing effect at high pressure described in Section 3.1 as the electrode is mechanically compressed and coating detachment potentially hindered. The graphite and silicon fractions therefore age at the same rate, which is consistent with the observations from the SEM images and the swelling analysis and could therefore be caused by pore blockage and microstructural degradation. Li plating could also or additionally explain this different aging behavior compared to the softly braced cell, as it can be assumed that it is more likely to occur with increased pressure and the observed electrode densification. Li plating would lead to additional capacity losses and an increase in cell thickness [65,66], which would explain both the observed very strong aging and the pressure development. Compared to Fig. 6c, the peaks in Fig. 6d flatten with further capacity loss due to increased inhomogeneities in the progress of the degradation, making the quantification of the silicon–graphite capacity ratio more difficult. Inhomogeneous aging due to a high pressure increase, including a hotspot as a result of compressed electrodes or separators, has previously been described in the aging of silicon-containing cells [10,54].

In general, the results of the SiO
x
34 cell analysis shown in Fig. 6e–f are qualitatively similar to those of the SiO
x
22 cell. However, the phenomena observed in the SiO
x
22 cell were even more pronounced due to the higher silicon content: In the SiO
x
34 cell, the capacity share of graphite at BoL was approximately 37% of the cell capacity, while the SiO
x
accounted for approximately 63%. The strong influence of the bracing condition is visible in Fig. 6d, as the curves drift apart, particularly when the curves are compared to the graphite cell (Fig. 6b). The reversible particle expansion transferred to the electrode structure (shown in Fig. 3o) is greatest for SiO
x
34 particles, resulting in the fastest degradation compared to the other silicon-containing active materials. This is especially the case at higher pressures, as a larger fraction of the particles will be forced into the pores, resulting in an increase in interparticle stress.

In principle, the observed and proposed aging root causes also apply to the Si-C 34 cell shown in Fig. 6g–h. This proves once more that the weak point of the cells investigated in this study is the mechanical integrity of the electrode structure (e.g. the electrical/ionic particle connection) rather than the particle itself or the applied active material technology (SiO
x
vs. Si-C).

As shown in Fig. 7a–b, the energy density evolution was strongly affected as the capacity deteriorated, while the cell volume changed simultaneously. Fig. 7a shows the decreasing energy density over SoH in the soft bracing condition for all investigated chemistries, based on the measurements shown in Fig. 2. The pressure was equally low for all investigated cells in the soft bracing (approximately 100–300 kPa).

Despite increased swelling, the silicon-containing cells maintained higher energy density compared to the graphite cell, while the SiO
x
34 cell offered the highest energy density overall. The Si-C 34 cell performed slightly worse compared to SiO
x
34 due to higher initial capacity loss and worse swelling. Considering overall energy density only, the Si-C 34 performed similarly to the SiO
x
22 cell. Regarding cycling performance, although a similar energy throughput could be reached at optimum operating pressure, the achievable cycles were slightly fewer compared to the SiO
x
22 cell. Thus, the theoretical energy density advantage of Si-C 34 could not be utilized when the cell is only slightly compressed due to its higher swelling (see Fig. 2l). Due to swelling over the lifetime, the Si-C 34 cell even intersected the SiO
x
22 cell at approximately 85% SoH and then performed slightly worse. However, as these are prototype cells, an influence of the tolerance variations, particularly on the cell and electrode thicknesses and thus on the energy density, cannot be completely ruled out. In contrast to the SoH, the energy density shown in Fig. 7c–d can no longer be approximated by a linear function when plotted against the ETP, as the energy density developed non-linearly. It is clear from the diagram that the energy density advantage of the silicon-containing cell chemistries only existed up to a certain number of cycles if the service life (ETP) is considered. Above a certain energy throughput, this energy density advantage was lost and graphite had a higher energy density. This becomes clear from the intersection of the graphite curve with the aging curves for cells containing silicon. In the case studied, this relationship applies regardless of the bracing (soft vs. stiff).

As can be seen in Fig. 7e–f, the energy density was significantly influenced by the stiff bracing which has been observed previously in the literature as well [22,54]. The energy density improvement at BoL compared to graphite is shown in Fig. 7e. Despite the high silicon content of 22% and the associated challenges for the service life and integration of the battery, an increase in energy density of only around 3% could be achieved. The pressure sensitivity of the SoC stroke and irreversible swelling (shown in Fig. 3i–l and m–p) helped to significantly improve the overall energy density. As shown in Fig. 7f, due to the greater swelling at EoT, the increase is more for the silicon-containing cells and particularly high for Si-C at more than 12%. It has already been analyzed in detail in the previous sections that the high pressure development of the rigid bracing has an extremely negative effect on the lifetime of the cell (Section 3.1). In addition, the increase relates to the comparatively low energy density of the Si-C 34 cell chemistry with soft bracing. However, the high pressure could be acceptable in an application scenario where battery life is of secondary importance. In such an application scenario, the simultaneous improvement in IR due to high pressure (see Fig. 3f–h) also has a positive effect on the performance. All influencing variables on the energy density are clearly shown again for BoL and EoT as well as for soft and stiff bracing in S.12 in Appendix A.

However, this overview does not take into account the better cyclic performance of graphite, which results in a much longer service life in terms of ETP at a similar SoH (shown in Fig. 7c–d). As shown in Fig. 8, this ultimately means that the energy density of graphite is again better than that of cells containing silicon at high cycle numbers. While the pressure remained advantageous after 100 cycles for all cells, its effect on the energy density became negative when the maximum number of cycles had been achieved. The root cause was the combination of high swelling and the negative influence of pressure on aging, as analyzed in depth in Sections 3.1.2 and 3.2 which leads to self-reinforcing aging behavior and thus to sudden cell death. Graphite, on the other hand, is much more cycle stable and shows no negative effects from pressure development even after 700 cycles. This shows that the mechanical design of a battery must be very careful and tailored to the application, as the energy density benefits that can be achieved by external pressure can also be lost by the same.

In this study, the influence of different mechanical resistance levels on the swelling, pressure evolution, and aging of silicon-containing cells with graphite composite anodes was investigated. Two different silicon technologies (SiO
x
and Si-C), as well as two silicon-compound weight shares in the anode (22 and 34 wt% SiO
x
), were tested. The relationship between pressure, swelling and aging behavior was thus demonstrated.

Within the systematic variation of pressure, an optimal pressure range between approximately 100 and 300 kPa was identified for all cell chemistries investigated. The graphite fraction dominates during irreversible swelling in the composite anode, while the silicon fraction dominates especially during reversible swelling. It appears that interparticle stress at the electrode level damaged the microstructure over the long term due to irreversibly growing particles. The effect was intensified by the pressure, the number of cycles and elemental silicon content. At stiff bracing, the effect was self-reinforcing, as the pores became increasingly clogged and more SEI was formed due to microstructural degradation, leading to sudden-death behavior in cell aging. From these results, it can be concluded that the electrode microstructure must be optimized for future anodes, especially those with a higher silicon content. In addition, it clearly shows the importance of further efforts to reduce swelling behavior at the particle level, regardless of whether SiO
x
or Si-C is used.

A future goal is to optimize the electrode microstructure of silicon-containing anodes to improve cycling performance even at higher levels of silicon content. In this way, the energy density potentials described above could also be exploited over a longer battery life at increased pressure. Such efforts should focus on the electrical/ionic bonding of the graphite particles, the expansion into the electrode structure should be reduced (e.g. by reducing the particle expansion), or the pore volume should be increased. Further research based on this publication will assist in improving the design of next-generation battery systems to produce practical advances, for example by increasing the range and performance of electric vehicles.
